content.title: Continuous Functions Are Integrable
content.content: If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, then $f$ is Riemann integrable.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Since $f$ is continuous on the compact set $[a, b]$, it is uniformly 
continuous. Given $\varepsilon > 0$, choose $\delta > 0$ such that 
$|x - y| < \delta$ implies $|f(x) - f(y)| < \varepsilon/(b - a)$.

Take any partition $P$ with $\|P\| < \delta$. On each subinterval 
$[x_{i-1}, x_i]$, we have $M_i - m_i < \varepsilon/(b-a)$.

Thus $U(f, P) - L(f, P) = \sum (M_i - m_i)(x_i - x_{i-1}) < \varepsilon$.

content.title: Monotone Functions Are Integrable
content.content: If $f$ is monotone on $[a, b]$, then $f$ is Riemann integrable.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Assume $f$ is increasing. For the uniform partition with $n$ subintervals, 
$M_i = f(x_i)$ and $m_i = f(x_{i-1})$. Then:
\[
U(f, P) - L(f, P) = \frac{b-a}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (f(x_i) - f(x_{i-1})) = \frac{(b-a)(f(b) - f(a))}{n} \to 0.
\]

content.title: Bounded Functions with Finitely Many Discontinuities
content.content:
If $f$ is bounded on $[a, b]$ and continuous except at finitely many points, 
then $f$ is Riemann integrable.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Linearity of the Integral
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are integrable on $[a, b]$, then:

1. $\int_a^b (f + g) = \int_a^b f + \int_a^b g$

2. $\int_a^b cf = c\int_a^b f$ for any constant $c$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Order Properties
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are integrable on $[a, b]$:

1. If $f(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$, then $\int_a^b f \geq 0$.

2. If $f(x) \leq g(x)$ for all $x$, then $\int_a^b f \leq \int_a^b g$.

3. $\left|\int_a^b f\right| \leq \int_a^b |f|$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Additivity over Intervals
content.content:
If $f$ is integrable on $[a, b]$ and $a < c < b$, then
\[
\int_a^b f = \int_a^c f + \int_c^b f.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Mean Value Theorem for Integrals
content.content:
If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, there exists $c \in [a, b]$ such that
\[
\int_a^b f = f(c)(b - a).
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
By EVT, $f$ attains its min $m$ and max $M$ on $[a, b]$. Then 
$m(b-a) \leq \int_a^b f \leq M(b-a)$. So $\frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f$ is 
between $m$ and $M$. By IVT, $f(c)$ equals this value for some $c$.
